overall_summary.headline: Oil and Fed optionality drove a front-end selloff while credit stayed active at a higher hurdle rate.
bullets: UST2Y +7 bp, UST5Y +5 bp, UST10Y +2 bp.
Listed credit funds fell while large financings still cleared.
Equities rose, but oil, DXY, and VIX tightened FI tone.

reader_takeaways.label: NY Close
reader_takeaways.title: Front end led the selloff
reader_takeaways.summary: UST2Y and UST5Y led while UST30Y held 4.99%.

reader_takeaways.label: Policy
reader_takeaways.title: Oil turned into Fed optionality
reader_takeaways.summary: Higher crude and Fed credibility repriced policy risk.

reader_takeaways.label: Credit
reader_takeaways.title: Supply was active but price-sensitive
reader_takeaways.summary: Borrowers cleared, but concessions and order books mattered.

overall_summary.ny_close_causal_narrative: The NY close was a front-end-led repricing: oil lifted inflation compensation, Fed optionality amplified the move, and active credit supply faced a higher financing hurdle.

top_drivers.title: Oil shock drove a front-end selloff
top_drivers.short_summary: WTI surged and the Treasury curve bear-flattened as markets repriced inflation pressure and Fed hike risk.
top_drivers.detail: UST2Y rose 7 bp to 4.05%, UST5Y rose 5 bp to 4.18%, UST10Y rose 2 bp to 4.47%, and UST30Y was flat near 4.99%. WTI jumped 5.49% and Brent rose 3.18%, while 10Y breakevens moved up to about 240 bp. That made the close an inflation-led front-end repricing rather than a simple risk rally.
top_drivers.market_move: UST2Y +7 bp, UST5Y +5 bp, BE10Y +2 bp, WTI +5.49%.
top_drivers.transmission: Higher crude lifted inflation compensation and pushed the front end toward Fed hike optionality.
top_drivers.confirmation: The 2s10s curve narrowed 5 bp while UST30Y was flat, confirming a front-end and belly-led repricing.

top_drivers.title: Fed credibility and AI capex kept hike risk alive
top_drivers.short_summary: Powell credibility comments and AI investment inflation risk reinforced the idea that the Fed could not lean quickly toward cuts.
top_drivers.detail: The news flow emphasized Powell warning that the Fed would lose credibility if officials could be dismissed over policy disagreements, while Apollo argued that AI data-center buildout could be inflationary early on. Traders were described as assigning about an 80% chance to a Fed hike this year.
top_drivers.market_move: The front end sold off while traders were described as assigning high odds to a Fed hike this year.
top_drivers.transmission: Powell credibility language and AI data-center inflation risk reduced the room for quick cuts.
top_drivers.confirmation: Flat real yields and wider breakevens made the policy channel inflation-led rather than a pure real-yield reset.

top_drivers.title: Credit weakened as financing supply stayed active
top_drivers.short_summary: Listed credit funds fell with rates, but primary markets remained busy in AI, M&A, BDC, and loan-extension channels.
top_drivers.detail: LQD, HYG, JNK, and EMB were all down on the day. At the same time, QXO launched a USD 3 billion junk-note sale, FS KKR sold USD 900 million of bonds, a CoreWeave-linked data center sought USD 850 million, and Citadel Securities marketed a larger 2033 loan. The signal was active supply against a higher hurdle rate.
top_drivers.market_move: LQD fell 0.39%, HYG fell 0.59%, JNK fell 0.62%, and EMB fell 0.44% while large financings remained active.
top_drivers.transmission: Higher Treasury yields raised the hurdle rate for M&A, BDC, data-center, and loan-extension supply.
top_drivers.confirmation: QXO, FS KKR, CoreWeave-linked financing, and Citadel Securities showed active but price-sensitive capital access.

watch_events.title: Oil and breakeven follow-through
watch_events.watch_line: Oil and breakevens must stop rising to stabilize the front end.
watch_events.market_trigger: WTI holds near the low 90s or rises while 10Y breakevens stay around or above 2.40%.
watch_events.market_implication: Persistent oil and breakeven pressure would keep 2Y and 5Y yields vulnerable.
watch_events.why_it_matters: The rates thesis depends on whether crude and inflation compensation confirm or reverse the close.
watch_events.confirms_if: Crude retraces and 10Y breakevens fall back below 2.40%.
watch_events.challenges_if: Oil extends higher and breakevens widen despite stable real yields.

watch_events.title: Fed reaction-function repricing
watch_events.watch_line: Fed communication decides whether the market keeps pricing hike optionality after the oil shock.
watch_events.market_trigger: Fed speakers emphasize credibility, political independence, or inflation risks from energy and AI capex.
watch_events.market_implication: Hawkish communication would keep the curve flatter and challenge risk assets that treated AI as purely growth-positive.
watch_events.why_it_matters: Front-end risk depends on whether policymakers validate hike optionality or allow relief pricing to extend.
watch_events.confirms_if: Fed language allows oil shock risk to fade without leaning against easier conditions.
watch_events.challenges_if: Fed language keeps hikes live while oil remains elevated.

watch_events.title: Credit primary-market clearing
watch_events.watch_line: Primary concessions show whether active supply still clears.
watch_events.market_trigger: M&A, BDC, data-center, or loan-extension deals require wider spreads or weaker covenants to clear.
watch_events.market_implication: Larger concessions would turn active supply from resilience into tighter financing conditions.
watch_events.why_it_matters: Order-book quality and concessions will show whether higher rates are changing borrower access.
watch_events.confirms_if: Deals clear with stable concessions and broad demand despite higher front-end yields.
watch_events.challenges_if: Borrowers delay deals or pay materially wider spreads.
watch_events.sensitive_channel: HY spreads, leveraged loans, concessions, and refinancing calendars.

cross_asset_cards.title: Credit

cross_asset_cards.title: Dollar / gold

long_thesis.english_markdown:
## Opening Fixed-Income Thesis

The June 1 close was a front-end-led fixed-income selloff driven by renewed oil pressure and a still-hawkish Fed distribution. UST2Y rose 7 bp to 4.05%, UST5Y rose 5 bp to 4.18%, UST10Y rose 2 bp to 4.47%, and UST30Y was flat near 4.99%. That pattern matters. It was not a parallel duration concession; it was a bear flattening in which the front end and belly absorbed the inflation and policy shock while the long bond stayed near the 5% area.

Oil was the immediate pricing channel. WTI jumped 5.49% to 92.16 and Brent rose 3.18% to 94.98 as US-Iran talks stalled and energy markets repriced the risk that higher crude would feed into inflation. The 10Y breakeven rose 2 bp to about 240 bp, while 10Y TIPS were flat near 2.07%. That split is important: nominal yields rose because inflation compensation increased, not because real yields alone reset higher. The rates market was responding to a price-level shock.

The policy context amplified the move. Powell's comments in the news flow focused on Fed credibility and the damage that would follow if officials could be removed over policy disagreements. Apollo's Torsten Slok argued that AI data-center investment would likely be inflationary in its early phase, delaying any push for quick rate cuts. The same reports described traders assigning roughly an 80% chance to a Fed hike this year. That gave the oil move a direct front-end transmission channel.

Credit and equities did not deliver one simple signal. SPX rose 0.26%, NDX 0.60%, and SOX 1.06%, helped by AI momentum, but VIX also rose 4.76%. Credit total-return prices weakened: LQD fell 0.39%, HYG 0.59%, JNK 0.62%, and EMB 0.44%. The dollar rose 0.29%, and gold fell 1.87%. This was not a classic risk-off session, because technology equities still worked. It was a fixed-income tightening day in which higher rates and energy pressure mattered more for credit than equity-index direction alone.

The broad thesis is that June 1 reintroduced the inflation shock into the center of the tape. The Fed path, breakevens, oil, and global duration all pointed toward a market that could not treat the previous oil relief as durable. Equities could rally on AI, but the fixed-income message was stricter: the hurdle rate for borrowers was rising again.

## UST Rates, Curve, Fed Pricing, Supply, Term Premium, And Liquidity

The Treasury curve was the clearest close signal. UST2Y rose 7 bp, UST5Y rose 5 bp, UST10Y rose 2 bp, and UST30Y was unchanged. The 2s10s spread narrowed to 42 bp, down 5 bp, and 2s30s narrowed to 94 bp, down 7 bp. This front-end and belly-led move fits a market repricing Fed optionality rather than term premium alone. If supply or long-end duration had been the center of the day, UST30Y would have led the selloff. It did not.

Fed credibility was not a side story. Powell warned that the central bank would lose the credibility required to support a strong and stable economy if presidents could remove officials over policy disagreements. That kind of institutional-risk language matters for rates because it defines how investors price the reaction function. A central bank defending credibility in an inflation shock is less likely to validate rapid easing, especially when energy and AI investment are both being described as inflationary.

AI capex reinforced the front-end risk. The news flow noted that the largest US technology firms planned as much as USD 725 billion of capital expenditures this year, mostly tied to data-center equipment, and that early AI buildout could add to inflation rather than reduce it. That directly challenged the idea that productivity gains would immediately give the Fed room to cut. In fixed-income terms, AI was not only an equity growth story; it was also a demand, power, construction, and financing story that could keep rates higher.

Inflation compensation confirmed the rates move. BE10Y rose 2 bp to around 240 bp, while TIPS10Y was flat. The nominal selloff therefore had an inflation-premium component. The 5Y breakeven was still around 242 bp, but the front-end Treasury move showed investors were more sensitive to the near-term policy implications of oil and AI-driven cost pressure than to long-horizon disinflation arguments.

The June 1 curve also changed the credit read. A 7 bp rise in the two-year yield and a 5 bp rise in the five-year yield tighten funding math immediately for floating-rate borrowers, new-issue concessions, and refinancing discussions. That is why listed credit funds could fall even as equities rose. The equity market was rewarding AI earnings and growth expectations; the rates market was charging borrowers for the same AI investment cycle.

The intraday Treasury detail from the news flow also matters. Yields were described as higher by one to four basis points in New York afternoon trading after having climbed as much as five to nine basis points across maturities earlier in the session, when US benchmark oil surged more sharply. That arc makes the close look like a partial stabilization after an oil-led rates shock, not a benign day. The front end still kept most of the repricing because it is the point most directly exposed to Fed optionality.

The stated market probability of a Fed hike this year, roughly 80%, gave the move its edge. If investors already believe a hike is likely, the curve can absorb bad oil news through two-year and five-year yields quickly. That is what happened. The 30-year yield did not need to move much because the first-order question was not the long-run fiscal path; it was whether the Fed would need to validate tighter policy in response to a fresh inflation impulse.

The direct supply and market-functioning evidence was mostly outside the US Treasury auction channel. South Korea intensified monitoring of its government bond market through daily calls and a messaging group with dealers and asset managers, explicitly responding to rising yields and investor positioning. That is a market-functioning fact. It shows an authority trying to contain disorderly moves in a domestic duration market as inflation and policy pressure rise.

South Korea matters for the global duration read because it demonstrates how quickly higher yields can become a policy concern outside the US. The news flow also showed Korean inflation accelerating to 3.1%, the fastest in more than two years, which reinforces the central bank's hawkish tilt. When a market has both faster inflation and official monitoring of bond conditions, investors should treat duration supply and liquidity as conditional on policy management rather than as free-running demand.

Chile provided a different supply and risk-premium signal. Investors were moving back into short-term peso bonds as Middle East inflation fears faded locally, and the cost of default insurance plus the extra yield on Chilean bonds over US counterparts had returned near pre-war levels. That is a positive local-market functioning fact, but it sits awkwardly beside the US oil move on June 1. It tells readers that EM local curves were not all responding to the same oil impulse at the same speed.

Corporate liquidity evidence came through restructuring rather than broad market plumbing. Atalian creditors agreed to take control of the firm, convert a large portion of liabilities into equity, extend remaining debt, and provide up to EUR 140 million of new super-senior financing. That does not speak to Treasury supply, but it shows capital was available when investors could reshape the balance sheet and improve seniority. The broader supply conclusion is that markets were active, but higher rates forced more explicit concessions and protections.

For US rates, the proportional conclusion is straightforward: the Treasury move itself was a price reaction to oil and Fed risk, not evidence about auction demand. The usable liquidity evidence was that other markets were either being monitored, repriced, or restructured to handle higher yields. That supports a higher term-premium and credit-risk discussion without overstating what the Treasury close alone proves.

India widened the global supply lens. Its benchmark 10-year yield had risen about 34 bp to around 7% since the Iran war began, and local banks expected further upside if rate-hike risk and fiscal concerns persisted. Fuel and fertilizer subsidies were cited as possible pressure points for public finances. That mix is highly relevant for term premium because it combines imported energy costs, fiscal burden, and domestic policy uncertainty.

The UK housing and Turkey growth facts made the same point through the activity channel. UK house prices fell 0.6%, the first decline of the year and worse than expected, as mortgage affordability remained stretched. Turkey's economy grew only 0.1% quarter over quarter, missing estimates, while the central bank was tightening to defend against Iran-related risks. These are not US Treasury supply facts, but they show how higher global rates and energy costs were already constraining activity in rate-sensitive sectors.

## Credit Conditions: IG, HY, Issuance, Refinancing, And Spread Confirmation

Credit weakened in total-return terms, but the market did not close. LQD fell 0.39%, HYG fell 0.59%, JNK fell 0.62%, and EMB fell 0.44%. Because Treasury yields rose, these listed fund moves cannot be translated mechanically into spread widening. Still, the direction was consistent with a higher hurdle rate for risk, especially after oil pushed the front end and belly higher.

The borrower-level evidence showed active supply. QXO started the bond portion of a USD 6 billion acquisition-financing package for TopBuild, including USD 3 billion of junk notes. FS KKR sold USD 900 million of bonds in a rare junk-rated offering by a publicly traded credit fund, with reported demand well above the initial target and pricing near the lower end of early talk. A CoreWeave-linked data center sought USD 850 million of junk-bond financing. Citadel Securities marketed a larger loan due in 2033 after record trading revenue.

This is not a closed-credit-market tape. It is a tape in which supply was active because borrowers wanted to lock capital, extend maturities, refinance construction costs, or finance M&A while investors still had appetite for yield. But the terms mattered. AI infrastructure deals were large and strategically important, yet they added future refinancing supply. M&A debt needed coupons and maturity structures that would clear a higher-rate environment. Trading-firm loans could lean on strong revenue, but still had to offer spread and discount protection.

EM credit was also differentiated. Colombian dollar bonds rose across the curve after Abelardo de La Espriella advanced to the presidential runoff, with the peso posting its best day since 2022 and swaps plunging. Chilean risk premia had moved back toward pre-war levels. Zambia, by contrast, faced creditor opposition to a USD 1.36 billion bond buyback. Those facts argue for country-specific policy and restructuring risk, not one broad EM credit verdict.

The correct June 1 credit conclusion is that higher rates hurt total returns and raised the cost of capital, but primary markets still had depth for stories with collateral, sponsorship, growth, or policy catalysts. The danger was not immediate market closure. It was that active supply was arriving just as the front end repriced higher.

The QXO financing showed M&A supply returning with size. A USD 6 billion debt package split between loans and bonds is meaningful even before final pricing, because it tells investors that acquisition finance was still moving through the market. The test is whether order books support the spread and maturity structure without forcing wider concessions after the Treasury front end has just repriced higher.

FS KKR provided a different credit test. The publicly traded credit fund increased its junk-bond sale to USD 900 million from an initial target of at least USD 400 million, with reported demand of about USD 1.5 billion and a 7.5% yield near the low end of initial talk. That is a strong demand signal for income product, but it is also a reminder that buyers were being paid meaningfully for risk at a time when benchmark yields were rising.

The CoreWeave-linked financing connected AI equity enthusiasm to leveraged-credit supply. Elk Grove Village Property sought USD 850 million of junk bonds for a hyperscale data center leased to CoreWeave, with proceeds intended to refinance existing debt and fund remaining construction. This turns AI from an index-performance story into a credit-underwriting story. The lease, construction schedule, power availability, and refinancing plan matter as much as the broad AI theme.

Europe and Citadel added still more evidence of active but selective credit. European leveraged-loan borrowers were trying to shave 25 bp to 50 bp from loans, while Citadel Securities sought to upsize and extend a loan to 2033 after record trading revenue. These are good-market behaviors, but they also rely on investor confidence that earnings or collateral can support longer-dated obligations. June 1 did not close that window; it made the window more rate-sensitive.

The Colombian rally was the clearest example of credit dispersion. Dollar bonds rose across the curve after Abelardo de La Espriella advanced to the runoff, with 2054 notes rising more than 4 cents to around 112 cents on the dollar. The peso gained 3.6%, its best day since 2022, and the equity benchmark rose as much as 7.1% before trimming gains. This did not offset the broader rates shock, but it showed that policy catalysts could still dominate local spread and FX performance.

Zambia represented the opposite side of EM credit. A creditor group opposed the government's tender for USD 1.36 billion of 2053 bonds, arguing that the terms were materially adverse and could trigger a clean-up call that would force remaining holders out of future upside. That is not a market-wide liquidity failure, but it is a strong reminder that restructuring mechanics and creditor rights can overwhelm beta when sovereign balance-sheet negotiations become contentious.

The credit conclusion must stay in that hierarchy. LQD, HYG, JNK, and EMB were useful total-return gauges for the rates-plus-risk move, but they did not prove broad spread widening by themselves. The stronger evidence came from QXO, FS KKR, CoreWeave-linked financing, Citadel Securities, and loan-extension activity: borrowers could still raise or extend capital, but the hurdle rate, concession, and refinancing mix mattered more after the front end sold off.

## DM Global Duration

Europe added another inflation channel. Isabel Schnabel argued that the ECB could no longer look through the Iran war's inflationary impact because price pressures were spreading beyond energy and the risk of de-anchored expectations was rising. Euro-area consumers' three-year inflation expectations eased slightly to 2.9%, but one-year expectations remained at 4% and five-year expectations at 2.4%, above the ECB's 2% target. That is not a backdrop in which non-US duration can provide easy relief for Treasuries.

Japan's yen story carried a similar message. Record intervention had bought time but had not changed the underlying rate differential with the US, and investors were focused on whether the BOJ would deliver a June hike. A wide differential, higher oil, and renewed inflation risk are exactly the ingredients that can keep pressure on a currency even when authorities are active. For US Treasuries, that means Japanese demand cannot be taken for granted.

The ECB added a policy-credibility channel. Schnabel's warning that the ECB could no longer look through the Iran war's inflationary impact put European rates on the same broad side as US front-end pricing. Even though consumer expectations had eased slightly at the three-year horizon, one-year expectations near 4% and five-year expectations above target showed why officials were still uneasy. That is why June 1 was a global FI story, not only a US oil story.

Activity data also showed how the rates shock was biting. UK house prices fell 0.6%, the first decline of the year and more than expected, as mortgage affordability stayed stretched. Turkey's economy grew only 0.1% quarter over quarter, below every surveyed estimate, while its central bank was tightening to manage conflict-related risks. These facts made the global section less about generic confirmation and more about a real distribution: some markets faced inflation pressure, some faced growth pressure, and some faced both.

## EM Rates, FX, And Hard-Currency Credit

Global duration confirmed the US message. Japan's yen remained pressured by the wide US-Japan rate differential, with investors focusing on whether BOJ action could finally do more than intervention to stabilize the currency. India faced upward pressure on yields from rate-hike risk and fiscal worries, with the benchmark 10-year yield already up roughly 34 bp since the Iran war began. South Korea combined higher inflation, official bond-market monitoring, and a hawkish central-bank tilt.

EM hard-currency credit and local-market stories had their own dispersion. Colombia rallied on a political outcome that investors interpreted as market-friendly. Chilean peso bonds gained favor as some investors believed Middle East inflation risk had faded. Korean and Indian bond markets, however, showed the opposite pressure from inflation and policy risk. That dispersion is the practical reason to keep the global section separate from the US rates section: the US curve set the anchor, but local inflation, politics, and financing needs determined how each market traded.

Korea made the inflation spillover concrete. Consumer prices rose 3.1% in May, above the expected 2.9% and the fastest pace since early 2024, while core inflation also firmed. This came alongside official monitoring of the bond market and a more hawkish central-bank tilt. That combination is a direct global-duration signal: energy and currency pressure were forcing authorities to watch both inflation and market functioning at the same time.

India widened the rates story beyond developed markets. Its 10-year yield had risen about 34 bp to around 7% since the Iran war began, and local forecasts expected additional upside if rate-hike risk and fiscal pressure persisted. Fuel and fertilizer subsidies were cited as possible budget pressure points. That is the same oil shock passing through a different fiscal and monetary structure, which is why global duration could not be reduced to the US curve alone.

## Cross-Asset Confirmation

Cross-asset markets were split rather than simply risk-on. The AI trade pushed equities higher, with SOX up 1.06% and NDX up 0.60%. But VIX rose, the dollar rose, oil rose, and credit total returns fell. That is a coherent mixed tape: growth equity investors still rewarded AI momentum, while fixed-income investors demanded more compensation for energy-driven inflation and financing risk.

The checked market board confirmed that split. SPX rose 0.26%, NDX rose 0.60%, and SOX rose 1.06%, but VIX rose 4.77%, DXY rose 0.29%, WTI jumped 5.49%, Brent rose 3.18%, gold fell 1.87%, and credit listed fund total-return gauges all declined. That mix said equity growth appetite survived while fixed-income, currency, volatility, and credit channels priced a tighter inflation-and-financing backdrop. That separation matters because it prevents the equity rally from being mistaken for broad fixed-income easing when the same board showed higher volatility, a firmer dollar, weaker credit total returns, and a larger oil-driven inflation premium.

## Market Significance And Watchpoints

The first watchpoint after the June 1 close was crude. The market had just learned that oil relief could reverse quickly when US-Iran talks stalled. If WTI stayed near the low 90s or moved higher, the front end would remain exposed because breakevens and Fed hike pricing had already responded. If crude retraced, the rates selloff could stabilize, but only if inflation expectations did not keep drifting higher.

The second watchpoint was Fed credibility. Powell's warning about political interference and Slok's inflationary AI capex argument both pushed against rapid cuts. The relevant test was whether incoming data allowed the Fed to keep the option value of hikes without forcing a disorderly repricing in risk assets. A market already assigning high odds to a hike can absorb some hawkish language; it becomes more fragile if oil and wages confirm the inflation concern.

The third watchpoint was credit supply. QXO, FS KKR, CoreWeave-linked financing, European loan repricings, and Citadel Securities all showed that borrowers were still coming. The question was whether demand would remain strong if the two-year and five-year Treasury yields kept moving higher. New-issue concessions, order-book quality, and the balance between refinancing and new leverage would give a better signal than listed-fund moves alone.

The final watchpoint was global duration spillover. Korea's bond-market monitoring, BOJ risk, India fiscal pressure, and ECB inflation language all made the US move harder to isolate. If non-US curves continued to cheapen, US duration would need either lower oil or weaker growth data to rally sustainably. The June 1 close was therefore a warning shot: AI equity strength could coexist with a much less forgiving fixed-income market.

A fourth implication was that equity strength should not be mistaken for easier credit. The AI trade helped NDX and SOX, but the same investment cycle was generating large financing needs and potential inflation pressure. If AI capex keeps supporting equity multiples while also increasing power, construction, and debt demand, fixed income will price the theme differently from equities.

A fifth implication was that EM dispersion should remain high. Colombia rallied on politics, Chile looked more comfortable with local inflation risk, Korea and India faced higher-yield pressure, and Zambia had creditor friction. That mix argues against one broad EM conclusion. The next useful signal will be whether stronger stories can keep outperforming while the global front end prices more Fed risk.

A sixth implication was that the next credit signal would come from deal quality, not just deal volume. QXO, FS KKR, CoreWeave-linked financing, European loan repricing, and Citadel Securities all showed that supply could reach investors. The question after a front-end selloff was whether books would stay deep without materially wider concessions. If the same borrowers cleared well after the oil shock, credit risk appetite was more resilient than listed funds implied; if concessions widened, the rates move was already tightening conditions.

A seventh implication was that political and institutional credibility had become part of rates pricing. Powell's warning about Fed independence, Colombia's election-driven rally, Korea's official bond-market monitoring, and Zambia's creditor dispute were different stories, but each affected the compensation investors demanded for policy uncertainty. June 1 therefore left fixed income with a broad credibility test, not just an oil-price test.

The validation framework is therefore three-part: oil and breakevens decide whether the inflation impulse keeps feeding the front end; Fed credibility and AI-capex inflation debate decide whether cuts stay off the table; and credit primary-market clearing decides whether active supply is still being absorbed or requires larger concessions.

section_summaries.summary: Frames June 1 as an oil-driven front-end selloff rather than a parallel duration concession.
section_summaries.market_read: The front end and belly absorbed inflation and Fed risk while the long bond held near 4.99%.
section_summaries.why_it_matters: The opening anchors the NY close and keeps the later pre-market update separate.
section_summaries.next_check: Watch whether oil and breakevens keep validating the inflation-led selloff.

section_summaries.section_title: UST Rates, Curve, Fed Pricing, Supply, Term Premium, And Liquidity
section_summaries.summary: Combines yield moves, curve flattening, Fed optionality, supply, and market-functioning evidence in one UST channel.
section_summaries.market_read: UST2Y and UST5Y led the selloff, showing policy repricing mattered more than long-end supply alone.
section_summaries.why_it_matters: The rates channel explains how crude became a Fed-distribution and curve-shape event.
section_summaries.next_check: Track 2Y/5Y yields, 2s10s, breakevens, and signs of market-functioning stress.

section_summaries.section_title: Credit Conditions: IG, HY, Issuance, Refinancing, And Spread Confirmation
section_summaries.summary: Separates weaker listed credit-fund price moves from active borrower-level financing evidence.
section_summaries.market_read: Credit was weaker but not closed; large M&A, BDC, data-center, and loan deals still tested demand.
section_summaries.why_it_matters: The credit read depends on clearing levels and concessions, not listed fund moves alone.
section_summaries.next_check: Watch direct spreads, concessions, order books, and refinancing versus new-leverage mix.

section_summaries.summary: Separates ECB, BOJ, and UK duration pressure from EM and cross-asset confirmation.
section_summaries.market_read: Europe and Japan kept policy credibility and currency-rate differentials in focus while UK housing showed rate sensitivity.
section_summaries.why_it_matters: DM policy pressure can reinforce US front-end repricing even when each market has its own data mix.
section_summaries.next_check: Watch ECB rhetoric, BOJ hike expectations, yen pressure, and rate-sensitive activity data.

section_summaries.section_title: EM Rates, FX, And Hard-Currency Credit
section_summaries.summary: Reads EM through local inflation, fiscal pressure, currency risk, and hard-currency refinancing rather than generic beta.
section_summaries.market_read: Korea and India faced inflation/rate pressure while Colombia and Chile showed more constructive local stories.
section_summaries.why_it_matters: EM transmission was dispersed across policy, dollar funding, and local-market liquidity.
section_summaries.next_check: Track EM local yields, DXY, oil-sensitive subsidies, and hard-currency spread behavior.

section_summaries.summary: Tests equities, volatility, DXY, oil, gold, and credit funding tone.
section_summaries.market_read: AI equities rose, but VIX, DXY, oil, and funding tone tightened FI conditions.
section_summaries.why_it_matters: Cross-asset confirmation separates equity-growth appetite from FI inflation, currency, volatility, and financing stress.
section_summaries.next_check: Watch equity resilience against VIX, DXY, oil, and direct spreads.

section_summaries.summary: Turns the close into oil, Fed credibility, and credit-clearing validation tests.
section_summaries.market_read: The selloff remains live until inflation compensation, policy rhetoric, and primary-market concessions stabilize.
section_summaries.why_it_matters: Watchpoints prevent future tests from being mistaken for realized NY-close drivers.
section_summaries.next_check: Use oil/breakevens, Fed communication, and new-issue clearing as the next proof points.

methodology_note: Synthesized market analysis from market levels and reporting context. Forward Market Significance carries the next market checks.

items.title: Front-End Shock
items.summary: Start with the bear flattening and why oil transmitted most strongly into 2Y and 5Y yields.

items.title: Fed Optionality
items.summary: Then connect Powell credibility language and AI-capex inflation risk to the policy distribution.

items.title: Credit Clearing
items.summary: Finish with whether active primary supply can clear after listed-fund price moves weakened.

cards.label: Rates
cards.headline: The curve bear-flattened from the front end
cards.detail: UST2Y +7 bp and UST5Y +5 bp led the selloff.

cards.label: Credit
cards.headline: Credit weakened even as supply stayed active
cards.detail: listed fund weakness met active primary financing tests.

cards.label: Pre-market
cards.headline: Morning futures kept the close under pressure
cards.detail: Treasury and Stock-index futures softened after NY close.

cards.label: Forward Watch
cards.headline: Oil, Fed credibility, and credit concessions are the tests
cards.detail: Oil, policy language, and concessions decide stabilization.
